issue_title: Consider releasing new Python package names for major version changes
issue_body:
I've chatted with @HonzaKral about this in the past, but with the 5.X series unfortunately we are having a lot of issues getting the elasticsearch-py package 5.X versions to talk nicely to 1.X clusters so I wanted to re-surface this suggestion (whereas elasticsearch-py 2.X versions mostly talked fine to 2.X and 1.X clusters).

Context: If an organization has a large Python code base that needs to communicate with multiple versions of Elasticsearch, it's really hard to do with the current packaging strategy of elasticsearch-py because you can't install multiple versions of the same library in the same virtualenv. In the past we've gotten away with this because orgs were using other libraries (e.g. pyes) to communicate with 0.X and then elasticsearch-py for 1.X; so you could install both in the same virtualenv.

Proposal: I propose that since elasticsearch-py already does branch based development and uses relative imports, major version changes publish packages under a different package name, e.g. elasticsearch5. This way large python projects can import as many major versions of the client library as they want and not worry about cross version compatibility.

Tradeoffs: This solution mainly translates to some maintenance overhead in tests, where the code actually references the package name. Since elasticsearch-py already uses branches, this ought be relatively straightforward.

Alternatives: Last time I chatted with Honza he suggested two alternatives. The first is to just use the latest version of elasticsearch-py to talk to all versions and it would probably mostly work because the bodies are what change version to version. For Elasticsearch 2.X this worked out ok, but Elasticsearch 5.X appears to have introduced some gnarly if-else complexity around node status endpoints and the like [example](https://github.com/elastic/elasticsearch-py/pull/482/files) and our integration tests are definitely complaining about elasticsearch-py 5.X's ability to communicate with 1.X clusters. The second alternative he suggested was a 'compat' namespace using AddonClient. I think this alternative could definitely work, but I feel like there is a lot of maintenance overhead for customers running multiple versions (having to maintain the compatibility layer).

Totally cool with the answer being "let's just have the customers that need it do namespaces with AddonClient" or "we'll put the if-else logic in elasticsearch-py", but I just wanted to raise the suggestion again. I think there is a lot of good prior art in system packaging (apache vs apache2), java packaging (shaded jars and package relocation) and even Python packaging (boto2 vs boto3) to support the usefulness of changing the package name for major version changes.

issue_title: RPM name conflict
issue_body:
I create a RPM file according the Python [manual](https://docs.python.org/2/distutils/builtdist.html#creating-rpm-packages): 
```
> python setup.py bdist --formats=rpm
```
This command generate a RPM specification file which contains the same name like the 'real' Elasticsarch [RPM](https://artifacts.elastic.co/downloads/elasticsearch/elasticsearch-5.3.0.rpm) and cause a conflict when you install both RPM files.

```
> rpmbuild -bb elasticsearch.spec
> rpm -qip elasticsearch-5.3.0-1.noarch.rpm  | head
Name        : elasticsearch
Version     : 5.3.0
Release     : 1
Architecture: noarch
```

**Solution**
- Change the name in setup.py to 'python-elasticsearch'

issue_title: ACR Credential Helper for Docker and Maven Settings
issue_body:
**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**
An credential helper for Docker and Maven Settings file will ease the pain for developers

**Describe the solution you'd like**
This should be modeled after `[az aks get-credentials](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/cli/azure/aks?view=azure-cli-latest#az-aks-get-credentials)`

Credentials should be set for Docker CLI and Maven Settings file. Here is an example of how to put them in Maven settings file.
Example settings.xml:

```
<settings>
  ...
  <servers>
    ...
    <server>
      <id>MY_REGISTRY</id>
      <username>MY_USERNAME</username>
      <password>{MY_SECRET}</password>
    </server>
  </servers>
</settings>
```
The id field should be an identifier for a registry server these credentials are for.


**Describe alternatives you've considered**
Manual construction - painful and error prone.

issue_body:
Currently the curl install script refers to nightly builds (i.e. `
curl -L https://aka.ms/ProjectAzInstall | sudo bash`).

Once packages have been published to PyPI (https://github.com/Azure/azure-cli/issues/648), we should have an install script that uses this.

The server hosting the script should use HTTPS (https://github.com/Azure/azure-cli/issues/394) and the URL should also change.

issue_title: Make --subscription a global parameter for all commands
issue_body:
The current CLI currently requires this workflow:

```
# set subscription
az account set --subscription <sub_name>

# do az stuff in that sub
az acs login --name ...
```

This makes it very unfriendly and dangerous for scripts and interactive use.

Consider:

1. Long running script sets curernt sub to SubProduction, then does some azure commands.
2. In the meantime user from the terminal, is thinking the commands execute against SubDev issues "delete resource group MyApp". **OOPS**.

If we had `--subscription` as a globa flag, we could have all scripts use that and not interfere with each other.

issue_title: Private IP allocation for multiple VMs
issue_body:
Hi,
I am using the sdk to create multiple vms under the same resource group. The code for the same is as below. Despite of choosing the method of private ip allocation as dynamic,  it somehow seems to assign the same IP  for example :'10.1.0.4' to all the VMs. That does not work for my application as it uses private IPs to communicate internally amongst the multiple nodes. Also giving the address space and subnet address prefix is mandatory so I have to pass a range in and it somehow always chooses the same one from that range. Am I missing something or do I need to specify some parameter which chooses different IPs? Or do I need to configure the network interface any differently?

```python
print ("Creating/Updating the Virtual Network")
address_space = AddressSpace(address_prefixes=['10.1.0.0/16'])
subnet1 = Subnet(name=subnet_name, address_prefix='10.1.0.0/24')
result = network_client.virtual_networks.create_or_update(group_name, network_name, VirtualNetwork(location=region, address_space=address_space, subnets=[subnet1]))
self.sleep_until_update_operation_done(result, network_name, verbose)
subnet = network_client.subnets.get(group_name, network_name, subnet_name)

print ("Creating/Updating the Public IP Address")
ip_address = PublicIPAddress(location=region, public_ip_allocation_method=IPAllocationMethod.dynamic, idle_timeout_in_minutes=4)
result = network_client.public_ip_addresses.create_or_update(group_name,ip_name, ip_address)
self.sleep_until_update_operation_done(result, ip_name, verbose)
public_ip_address = network_client.public_ip_addresses.get(group_name, ip_name)

print ("Creating/Updating the Network Interface")
network_interface_ip_conf = NetworkInterfaceIPConfiguration(name='default', private_ip_allocation_method=IPAllocationMethod.dynamic, subnet=subnet, public_ip_address=PublicIPAddress(id=(public_ip_address.id)))
result = network_client.network_interfaces.create_or_update(group_name, interface_name, NetworkInterface(location=region,ip_configurations=[network_interface_ip_conf]))
self.sleep_until_update_operation_done(result, interface_name, verbose)
```

issue_title: Lower Pri: Large folder upload/download has a large startup cost in time
issue_body:
When uploading or downloading a very large directory, we see a large startup cost where the client is getting all the information it needs to begin (or resume) the operation. This could benefit from some parallelization or optimizations to reduce the amount of time needed to setup the operation.

The current timing is about 30 seconds for a folder with 10,000 1mb files in a nested structure.

issue_title: Adding projection matrix changes view angle
issue_body: It seems that when we compute a view and projection matrix, the view is correct in renderings if we do not include the projection matrix and incorrect when we do. A minimal example is available at https://github.com/kuanfang/debug-bullet. If you call example.py with argument 0, it renders an image using just the view matrix, and if you call it with 1, it renders using view and projection matrices. Please let me know if there is a fix that allows us to use our intrinsics along the correct line of sight. Thanks!!

issue_title: Critical bug in GMM EM algorithm
issue_body:
### Introduction
For quite a long time I've been struggling with the problem of too small variances and decreasing LL during GMMHMM training. Finally, I've found rather a big problem.
(You can skip to one-before-last section to see what's wrong in the code.)

### What we have now
The ``fit()`` method in ``_BaseHMM.py`` basically works as follows:
1. Accumulate sufficient statistics (ss) for every training example (E step)
2. Do M step
3. Check convergence

In the case of GMMHMM, ``_accumulate_sufficient_statistics()`` method uses ``sklearn.mixture.gmm``'s mechanisms to extract ss from training example. Namely, for each HMM state, it constructs a temporary GMM:

            tmp_gmm = GMM(g.n_components, covariance_type=g.covariance_type)
            tmp_gmm._set_covars(
                distribute_covar_matrix_to_match_covariance_type(
                    np.eye(n_features), g.covariance_type,
                    g.n_components))

And asks it to perform its M step:

            norm = tmp_gmm._do_mstep(X, gmm_posteriors, self.params)

Then, that GMM's parameters are converted to sufficient statistics. First and second moments (here, a little misleadingly, called ``stats['means']`` and ``stats['covars']``) are multiplied by null moment (called ``norm``):

            stats['norm'][state] += norm
            if 'm' in self.params:
                stats['means'][state] += tmp_gmm.means_ * norm[:, np.newaxis]
            if 'c' in self.params:
                if tmp_gmm.covariance_type == 'tied':
                    stats['covars'][state] += tmp_gmm.covars_ * norm.sum()
                else:
                    cvnorm = np.copy(norm)
                    shape = np.ones(tmp_gmm.covars_.ndim)
                    shape[0] = np.shape(tmp_gmm.covars_)[0]
                    cvnorm.shape = shape
                    stats['covars'][state] += tmp_gmm.covars_ * cvnorm

### And here is the problem. 
While it is ok to do so for first order moments, doing that for second order moments is an error! Means and ovariances of a given mixture of ``tmp_gmm`` are estimated in M step according to equations like these:

    new_means = first_moment / null_moment
    new_covars = second_moment / null_moment - new_means^2

So to "roll back" to moments from means and covars, one has to do:

    first_moment = new_means * null_moment  # this is done correctly in the current implementation
    second_moment = (new_covars + new_means^2) * null_moment  # and this is NOT!

### What we can do
A hotfix for this problem would be:

In ``hmm.py``, method ``_accumulate_sufficient_statistics()``, line ``610``:
change

    stats['covars'][state] += tmp_gmm.covars_ * cvnorm

to

    stats['covars'][state] += (tmp_gmm.covars_ + tmp_gmm.means_**2) * cvnorm

And in ``hmm.py``, method ``_do_mstep()``, line ``636`` (and respectively for full covariance matrix):
change

    g.covars_ = (stats['covars'][state] + self.covars_prior) / cvnorm

to

    g.covars_ = (stats['covars'][state] + self.covars_prior) / cvnorm - g.means_**2


### Final thoughts
All this leads to a doubt: do we really need to use ``sklearn.mixture.gmm``'s methods? Wouldn't it be easier and clearer if just wrote the EM algorithm for GMMHMM?

Regards
Michal

issue_title: Transmat normalization leads to incorrect adaptation (left-right HMMs)
issue_body:
Hello again

It seems to me that the problem related to #33 and #65 still exists. Transmat adaptation in left-right HMMs results in ergodic topology (zero probabilities are raised to non-zero).

in `base.py` we have:

    def _do_mstep(self, stats):
        (...)
        # The ``np.where`` calls guard against updating forbidden states
        # or transitions in e.g. a left-right HMM.
        (...)
        if 't' in self.params:
            transmat_ = self.transmat_prior - 1.0 + stats['trans']
            self.transmat_ = np.where(self.transmat_ == 0.0,
                                      self.transmat_, transmat_)
            ############################
            normalize(self.transmat_, axis=1)  # WE NORMALIZE AFTER CHECKING FOR ZEROS
            ############################

And in `utils.py` we have:
```
def normalize(a, axis=None):
    (...)
    ####################
    a += np.finfo(float).eps  # AND HERE WE LOOSE OUR "GUARD"
    ####################

    a_sum = a.sum(axis)
    if axis and a.ndim > 1:
        # Make sure we don't divide by zero.
        a_sum[a_sum == 0] = 1
        shape = list(a.shape)
        shape[axis] = 1
        a_sum.shape = shape

    a /= a_sum

    # TODO: should return nothing, since the operation is inplace.
    return a
```

issue_title: part.set_content_disposition is ignored
issue_body:
## Long story short

Using part.set_content_disposition for some multipart file stuff, couldn't get it to work.
So I checked the part header, which is as I expected and then made a fake writer to check what the payload being sent actually was. The content disposition in the payload doesn't at all match what I have set in the part.

## Expected behaviour

The content disposition in the payload is as set in the part.

## Actual behaviour

It appears to generate a fresh content disposition.

## Steps to reproduce

![Demo](https://i.dabbitpri.me/qq42y4inzq.png)
```py
class Writer:

    def __init__(self):
        self.buffer = ""

    async def write(self, stuff):
        self.buffer += stuff.decode('utf-8','ignore')

    def done(self):
        print(self.buffer[:300])

# Generate multipart
with aiohttp.MultipartWriter('form-data') as mp:
    part = mp.append(open(path, 'rb'))
    part.set_content_disposition(
        'form-data',
        quote_fields=False,
        name='files[]',
        filename=os.path.split(path)[1]
    )
    # Fix aiohttp being retarded
    part._headers['Content-Disposition'] = part._headers['Content-Disposition'].split(" filename*=utf-8", 1)[0]
    print("\n", part._headers['Content-Disposition'], "\n")
    w = Writer()
    await mp.write(w)
    w.done()
```

## Your environment

Aiohttp 3.2.1
Python 3.6
macOS 10.13.4

# The issue (as far as I can tell)

https://github.com/aio-libs/aiohttp/blob/master/aiohttp/multipart.py#L754-L755
It appears there that the payload headers are saved separately and so no matter what you do to the returned part after it's appended, the headers will never actually be updated in the multipart.

# Hotfix (until this issue is resolved)

Create your payload yourself, set headers etc. before you append to the multipart.
eg.
```py
# Hotfix
from multidict import CIMultiDict
payload = aiohttp.payload.get_payload(open(path, 'rb'), headers=CIMultiDict())
payload.set_content_disposition(
    'form-data',
    quote_fields=False,
    name='files[]',
    filename=os.path.split(path)[1]
)
payload._headers['Content-Disposition'] = payload._headers['Content-Disposition'].split(" filename*=utf-8", 1)[0]

# Add part to multipart
part = mp.append(payload)
```

issue_title: ClientResponse.connection becomes None too early
issue_body:
Now `ClientResponse._response_eof()` cleans up `self._connection` on getting end of response's BODY.
For relative small bodies the method is called before even very first `resp.read()`.

It works pretty well from content delivery perspective but raises another problem: there is no reliable way to get underlying socket options, in particular we need `transport.get_extra_info('peercert')`.

In theory aiohttp could collect all data before connection releasing but there is no public asyncio API for providing all available transport's keys.
Moreover I could imagine situation where direct call is needed like `tr.get_extra_info('socket').getsockopt(...)`.

I have no good proposal, keeping `resp.connection` until at least first `resp.read()` breaks pipelining if I understand correctly.
But I pretty sure the problem should be fixed.

@fafhrd91 please share your opinion.

issue_title: Make pool.release method return future so we can wait on it in __aexit__
Use loop.create_future() instead direct instantiation
issue_body: Recent asyncio added new function `loop.create_future()` for `Future` object creation, so third party loops like `uvloop` can create own faster future implementations. We need to use this approach to benefit from speed ups.

issue_title: matplotlib x axis labels dissapear in scatterplot
issue_body:
Please take look at http://stackoverflow.com/questions/43121584/matplotlib-scatterplot-x-axis-labels or https://github.com/Kornel/scatterplot-matplotlib/blob/master/Scatter%20plot%20x%20axis%20labels.ipynb 

When I create a scatter plot with the colours specified using `c="C"` the x axis labels disappear.

```
import pandas as pd
%matplotlib inline
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
%config InlineBackend.figure_format = 'retina'

test_df = pd.DataFrame({
        "X": [1, 2, 3, 4],
        "Y": [5, 4, 2, 1],
        "C": [1, 2, 3, 4]
    })

# This shows the x axis labels just fine
test_df.plot(kind="scatter", x="X", y="Y");

# This does not
test_df.plot(kind="scatter", x="X", y="Y", c="C");
```

The solution is to provide the axes explicitly:

```
fig, ax = plt.subplots()
test_df.plot(kind="scatter", x="X", y="Y", s=50, c="C", cmap="plasma", ax=ax);
```

This looks like a jupyter issue since calling this from "regular" python with savefig does not yield this problem.

issue_title: Rendering issues with IPython. Equations not properly renderd
issue_body:
Hi,
with **Ubuntu 17.04** and **Firefox 50.1 / Chrome 57.0** and **Jupyter 4.2.1** I get wrongly rendered equations. 

The surprising part is, that the second after I hit the `Enter` button, the rendering looks right. Then after some more seconds the renderer decides to mess everything up. Does this behavior sound familiar?

**BTW**: the github renderer looks right.

![logo](http://i.imgur.com/tqRHhH3.png)

issue_title: track last activity of kernels
issue_body:
It's proven useful in JupyterHub to track last activity on each notebook server and expose that on the API, so that external services can perform operations such as culling idle servers, etc. We could track similar information on a per-kernel basis in the single-user notebook.

Rough draft in my head:

- subscribe a single activity monitor SUB socket to all kernels' IOPub channels
- primitive version: update last_activity on each message, not bothering to deserialize
- cleverer version: use busy/idle, so that a silent but busy kernel doesn't appear as idle
- include last_activity timestamps in the models on the kernels (and thereby sessions) REST API

issue_title: default validation context should add a oo item
issue_body:
```python
from schematics.models import Model
from schematics.types import IntType, StringType
from schematics.types.compound import ModelType


class SomeModel(Model):
    intt = IntType(required=True)
    strt = StringType(required=True)
    
ModelType(SomeModel, required=True).validate({"intt":1, "strt": "abcd"})

```

See python code above. It would raise exception as following:
```python
.../schematics/types/compound.pyc in _convert(self, value, context)
    124             raise ConversionError(
    125                 "Input must be a mapping or '%s' instance" % self.model_class.__name__)
--> 126         if context.convert and context.oo:
    127             return model_class(value, context=context)
    128         else:

AttributeError: 'Context' object has no attribute 'oo'
```
After tracing back, it seems the default validation context misses an item "oo".

```python
.../schematics/validate.py

def get_validation_context(**options):
    validation_options = {
        'field_converter': validation_converter,
        'partial': False,
        'strict': False,
        'convert': True,
        'validate': True,
        'new': False,
--->    'oo': False
    }
    validation_options.update(options)
    return Context(**validation_options)
```

Inserting the line above solves the problem.

issue_title: Difficulties in creating an ECDSA key
issue_body:
I know PyKMIP doesn't support the "Cryptographic Domain Parameters" attribute which is used to send the preferred curve. But that is ok because (AIUI) in KMIP it is acceptable when creating an ECDSA key to just specify "Cryptographic Length" and allow the server to pick a curve of its preference. However that does not work because `_build_key_attributes` in `kmip/pie/client.py` adds ENCRYPT/DECRYPT params as default and included in the common ops:

```
        # Default crypto usage mask value
        mask_value = [enums.CryptographicUsageMask.ENCRYPT,
                      enums.CryptographicUsageMask.DECRYPT]
        if masks:
            mask_value.extend(masks)
```

In ECDSA case of course this is just invalid since ECDSA cannot encrypt, so the request is rejected. (Well, it is not really clear from KMIP spec what the server should do in the event of invalid usage mask, but rejecting the request seems not unreasonable.) It seems to me that if the user specified `public_usage_mask` and `private_usage_mask` then it is not right to also silently enable encrypt/decrypt.

I'm doing this:

```
    key_id = client.create_key_pair(                                                                                                                                                           
        enums.CryptographicAlgorithm.ECDSA,                                                                                                                                                    
        256,                                                                                                                                                                                   
        operation_policy_name='default',                                                                                                                                                       
        public_name='Test_ECDSA1_pub',                                                                                                                                                         
        public_usage_mask=[                                                                                                                                                                    
            enums.CryptographicUsageMask.VERIFY                                                                                                                                                
        ],                                                                                                                                                                                     
        private_name='Test_ECDSA1',                                                                                                                                                            
        private_usage_mask=[                                                                                                                                                                   
            enums.CryptographicUsageMask.SIGN                                                                                                                                                  
        ]                                                                                                                                                                                      
    )                                                                                                                                                                                          
```

issue_title: Await async finalizer to request
issue_body:
Hi,
My project is Python 3.5 only, and it's therefore impossible for me to use async generators in fixtures. But we might be able to workaround this if it were possible to define async finalizers to `request`.

E.g.
```python
@pytest.fixture
async def foo_fixture(request):
    async def fin():
        await some_stuff()
    request.addasyncfinalizer(fin)
    return 42
```
Then it would await for each async finalizers before tear down.

Not sure whether it would involve monkey patching on the request object, or what would be acceptable. Maybe there are also more proper solutions that doesn't involve `request` or that make use of already existing features? I already tried to use `loop.create_task()` in a standard finalizer, unfortunately, the created task seem to execute after the teardown which produces the following error message:
`2017-06-26 18:15:50,262 - asyncio - ERROR - Task was destroyed but it is pending!`

Many thanks!

issue_title: Input validation error in BenefitForm
issue_body:
### Issue Summary

By defining a custom incentive, the input validation of the offer creation allows to input values that, lead to an error. 
After this, the whole offers page of the dashboard is inaccessible, because of an error message.

### Steps to Reproduce

Prerequisite: Existing custom incentive

1. Start creating a new offer in the dashboard
2. Set random name, e.g. "Test"
3. For the incentive just select "Range" with any of the provided options and leave the rest blank
4. Submit the incentive
5. Fill the rest of the procedure with any data
6. Submit the offer
7. Dashboard shows an error, and offers page is no longer accessible without an error

### Technical details
Usage of sandbox version as described in the [docs][sandbox]. No further modifications needed.

```
$ python --version
Python 3.6.3
$ pip show django | grep Version
Version: 2.0.6
$ pip show django-oscar | grep Version
Version: 2.0.dev0
```

The problem in this case, is that [clean][clean-method] method of the BenefitForm, does only check if one of the values is set. By setting the range, this check is fulfilled.

### Fix
I submitted a simple PR #2762 for fixing this input validation error.


[sandbox]: https://django-oscar.readthedocs.io/en/latest/internals/sandbox.html#running-the-sandbox-locally
[clean-method]: https://github.com/django-oscar/django-oscar/blob/06323399b0dfdcbbf63606a3607ca9648a991cca/src/oscar/apps/dashboard/offers/forms.py#L137

issue_title: CentOS71 docker to use LLVM3.9
issue_body:
Thought I should share that I am able to build CoreCLR (and CoreRT native code) with the following dockerfile:

```dockerfile
FROM centos:7
# it should pick the latest in 7 (atm 7.3.1611)
RUN cat /etc/*-release

RUN yum -y -q  install epel-release
RUN yum-config-manager --add-repo https://copr.fedorainfracloud.org/coprs/alonid/llvm-3.9.1/repo/epel-7/alonid-llvm-3.9.1-epel-7.repo 
RUN yum -y -q update
RUN yum -y -q install \
      git cmake make icu libicu-devel lttng-ust lttng-ust-devel libuuid-devel \
      libunwind libunwind-devel llvm-3.9.1 clang-3.9.1 lldb-3.9.1 \
      lldb-3.9.1-devel --enablerepo=alonid-llvm-3.9.1

ENV PATH /opt/llvm-3.9.1/include:/opt/llvm-3.9.1/lib64:/opt/llvm-3.9.1/bin:$PATH

RUN git clone https://github.com/dotnet/coreclr -b master --single-branch \
    && cd coreclr \
    && ./build.sh
```

These are the minimum set of dependencies required to build the said repos.

However, the one [in the repo](https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/master/tools/dotnet-bootstrap/base/lab/containers/centos71/Dockerfile) seems to be using LLVM, Clang and LLDB version 3.6 and bit of extra dependencies.

issue_title: Include (non-API) docs in PyPI packages?
Client keeps trying to reinstall when run on Raspberry Pi running Raspbian
issue_body:
I am creating this issue as a record for other to find.

After first time installation, the client runs and after the line `Illegal instruction` is printed, begins recreating the virtual environment and reinstalling Python packages every time it is run.

This is caused by the version of `libaugeas` provided by Raspbian repositories not supporting the ARMv6 architecture. To confirm this::

    $ sudo apt-get install python-augeas
    $ python
    ...
    >>> import augeas
    Illegal instruction
    $

To work around this, you must remove `libaugeas` and replace it with one built from source::

    sudo apt-get remove libaugeas0 augeas-tools libaugeas-dev
    sudo apt-get install libreadline6-dev
    wget http://download.augeas.net/augeas-1.4.0.tar.gz
    tar -zxf augeas-1.4.0.tar.gz
    cd augeas-1.4.0
    CFLAGS='-march=armv6 -mfpu=vfp -mfloat-abi=hard' CPPFLAGS='-march=armv6 -mfpu=vfp -mfloat-abi=hard' ./configure
    CFLAGS='-march=armv6 -mfpu=vfp -mfloat-abi=hard' CPPFLAGS='-march=armv6 -mfpu=vfp -mfloat-abi=hard' make
    sudo make install

Then retry letsencrypt.

**Note that this approach installs an unmanaged version of libaugeas and future updates including security fixes will not be automatically provided as part of your normal system update procedure.**

issue_title: No exception raised when parsing datetime string with bogus year
issue_body:
The parser doesn't raise an exception when the year is the bogus value `000A`:

```
from dateutil.parser import parse

print(parse('000A-01-01T00:00:00'))  # 2018-03-15 00:00:00-01:00
```

Interestingly the parser *does* raise an exception with the bogus year `200A`, which is good:

```
from dateutil.parser import parse

parse('200A-01-01T00:00:00')  # ValueError: ('Unknown string format:', '200A-01-01T00:00:00')
```

I'm using `python-dateutil==2.7.0` with Anaconda Python 3.6.4 on macOS 10.11.6:

```
python -c 'import dateutil; print(dateutil.__version__)'  # 2.7.0
python --version  # Python 3.6.4 :: Anaconda custom (64-bit)
defaults read loginwindow SystemVersionStampAsString  # 10.11.6
```

I've also verified that the same behavior occurs with a09c9d65bd4a5fbc344593905bf81e175e3539c0, which is the tip of master at the moment.

I discovered this behavior after looking at #353 and fiddling with the formerly problematic input `201A-01-01T23:58:39.239769+03:00` mentioned there.

issue_title: Integrate custom.Types deeper in the library
issue_body:
> *This is a rather big change which probably needs more thought and is long-term.*

This would make it easier to use custom types everywhere accross the library. For instance, a `custom.Entity` that wrapped `User`, `Chat` and `Channel`. These entities can appear nearly everywhere, and replacing them by hand wherever a friendly method can return them is a pain.

Instead, `TLObject`'s could have a `def set_client(self, client)` or similar that they would override if they were aware of some special-cased objects. For instance, `messages.messageSlice` would know it has to set the client on its `messages`, `users` and and `chats`.

In the lower level, when the `BinaryReader` is about to read a known-special cased object, it would create an instance of that class. For instance, we could have:
```python
class Entity(TLObject):
    def online_within(self, ...):
        ...  # Convenience code

class User(Entity):  # Special to use the Entity
    ...
```

More than the reader, perhaps the code generator would need to know about this.

This would allow to get rid of the current hack for `custom.Message` to behave like either a `Message` or `MessageService`.

issue_title: Confusing error when trying to create a LinearColormap with wrong arg type

https://github.com/python-visualization/branca/blob/9f8f2fc395e6b6e4c22d291ca0da6a26f40562c3/branca/colormap.py#L51

This line raises an ``UnboundLocalError: local variable 'color_tuple' referenced before assignment`` when x is not an expected type, it's not very informative and makes it necessary to dig in the code to figure out the real problem.

```python
import seaborn as sns

from marshmallow import Schema   
               

class Square(object):

class SquareSchema(Schema):

        fields = ('x', 'y', 'surface')                                             
                                                                                   
                                                                                   
if __name__ == '__main__':

    serializer = SquareSchema()                                                    
    data = serializer.dump(s).data
    print data
 

````

getting surface
getting surface
getting surface

issue_title: Poor validator inheritance
issue_body:
The inheritance model for [`Range`](https://github.com/marshmallow-code/marshmallow/blob/dev/marshmallow/validate.py#L159) and its subclass [`Length`](https://github.com/marshmallow-code/marshmallow/blob/dev/marshmallow/validate.py#L201) does not seem semantically valid. This issue arose when [attempting to differentiate the two](https://github.com/marshmallow-code/apispec/pull/73) by duck typing the validators.

`Length` is not really a type of `Range`. `Range` validates numbers, and `Length` validates sequences and collections. This indicates that their domains are disjoint.

It would be more appropriate for the `Length` validator to have an attribute named something like `lengthRange` which is an instance of the `Range` validator.

This issue is equivalent to extending an `Engine` class to create a `Car` class. A `Car` has an `Engine`, but it is not a type of `Engine` itself.

issue_title: Validating value is unique
issue_body:
Hi

I have a user schema with an email field. The email is validated to be unique:

```
class UserSchema(ma.Schema):
    def validate_email(value):
        return User.query.filter(User.email == value).first() is None

    username = ma.fields.String()
    email = ma.fields.Email(validate=validate_email)
```

This works great when creating users, but when updating a user with same email address it fails because there is already a user with same email address: the same user as the one being updated!

The validator unfortunately only has access to the email value so I can't check if the user found is the same as the one being updated.

How do I best handle this?

issue_title: How to implement JSON API Compound Document
API example to update existing instance does not work for partial updates
issue_body:
Trying to update an existing instance as shown [here](https://marshmallow-sqlalchemy.readthedocs.io/en/latest/_modules/marshmallow_sqlalchemy/schema.html#ModelSchema) does not work for partial updates since it fails validation due to missing attributes.

Say the User model also has a required address field, and you execute this:

`existing_user = schema.load({'name': 'Bill'}, instance=User.query.first())`

This will fail validation since the only data that is passed to the validator is the 'name' field. The load function in marsmallow.schema will ultimately fail when calling _unmarshal since it verifies all of a schema's fields but only the name will validate. An easy fix is updating the data dict in the load function in marshmallow_sqlalchemy.schema when that function is passed an existing instance. This works fine (note I'm using data.to_dict() since my data is an ImmutableMultiDict:

```
    def load(self, data, session=None, instance=None, *args, **kwargs):
        """Deserialize data to internal representation.

        :param session: Optional SQLAlchemy session.
        :param instance: Optional existing instance to modify.
        """
        self.session = session or self.session
        self.instance = instance or self.instance
        if not self.session:
            raise ValueError('Deserialization requires a session')

        if instance is not None:
            instance_data = self.dump(instance).data
            instance_data.update(data.to_dict())
            data = instance_data

        ret = super(ModelSchema, self).load(data, *args, **kwargs)
        self.instance = None
        return ret

```

issue_title: Support for pickling functions within a class defined outside of __main__?
issue_body:
Cloudpickle's ability to serialize instantiated classes and their member functions is a huge advantage over cloudpickle alternatives. Unfortunately, this feature seems limited heavily to the check for **instantiated_class.__module__ == '__main__'**.

One hacky solution is to set __module__ to '__main__' in the class definition:
```class SomeFunClass(object):
    __module__ = '__main__'

    # Rest of the class definition```

But this could cause problems with any code that relies on a proper value for __module__. Is there another solution I'm not seeing?

issue_title: Different initialization method for weight and bias
logging the learning rates during training
issue_body:
Printing learning rate (and/or other hyper params) in logs is of great help for DL application development/debugging. This is the default behavior of both Torch and Caffe. 

In our current implementation, learning rate (along with other hyper params) is updated inside optimMethod (i.e. SGD). DistOptimizer is unaware of any changes of hyper parameters and thus unable to output any meaningful logs for debugging.  

We could either pass the logger into optimMethod for certain status output, or make optimize() function return a structure indicating the status.  If there're concerns about too many logs, we could put those logs in optimMethod in debug mode.

Hi, I have several questions on the input shape.
Some of them may be duplicate of other issues but I want to make it clear and comprehensive here.

1. Do I need to re-arrange user and item ids?
The first question is on actual id and index of sparse matrix. Suppose my data set has integer user id which does not start from 0 and not contiguous, like `[2,4,6,7,10]`. Do I need to re-arrange it to `[0, 1, 2, 3, 4]` for making input interaction matrix and feature matrix of `fit` method? I believe both index style give essentially the same sparse matrices.

2. Can I supply feature matrices some of whose ids did not appear in the interaction matrix?
Suppose there are 10 users (id=0-9) registered in my service. However only 5 (say id = `[1,3,5,7,9]`) have activities. Then the interaction matrix only contains data for those 5 users. Now can I just put the complete 10 user feature matrix to the `fit` method? I know this does not help accuracy. It's just for the convenience. I don't want to make a partial 5 user feature matrix from the complete 10 user matrix.

3.  Shape of feature matrix in `predict` method.

3.1 Suppose I want to make some prediction for user_id = 100, who has no past activity, only features.
As the doc says, `predict` method accepts `user_features (np.float32 csr_matrix of shape [n_users, n_user_features], `. Here, what is `n_users`?  1 or 100? In other words, Does this feature matrix only has one row or it's a matrix of row > 100 and at least row_100 has non zero values?

3.2 If I used paired style api of `predict` what feature matrix shape?
For example, `user_ids = [1,1,20,20,100]` and `item_ids = [0,1,0,2,5]`. What is`n_users`? 3? 5? or 100?

Lastly, I only mentioned on user id/ matrix. Is the above discussion also valid for item part?

Thank you.

issue_title: Roadmap required for long term support of Mobile Flat-UI Theme
issue_body:
I think we will eventually have to look at creating a new theme for Mopidy-Musicbox-Webclient. It currently makes use of [jQuery Mobile Flat-UI Theme](https://github.com/ququplay/jquery-mobile-flat-ui-theme), which hasn't been maintained in about a year.

The theme also only supports [jQuery 1.3.1 or earlier](https://github.com/ququplay/jquery-mobile-flat-ui-theme#current-branch-layout), which was [released on 10 April 2013](https://jquerymobile.com/changelog/).

As new devices and operating systems are released, it is likely that more and more things will break as technology moves on. So getting onto some sort of viable upgrade path longer term to avoid having to maintain all of the libraries ourselves will become a priority at some point.

Some notes and considerations on moving forward:

- whatever changes we make in this area, it should continue to maintain the project ethos of simplicity and usability that was established by Wouter and which made Musicbox so popular.

- a lot of people really like Mopidy-Musicbox-Webclient, so reusing all of the best elements of the look & feel and user interaction model is probably the way to go.

- since merging this will be a lot of work, any changes should just be focussed on the theme elements as a start, and not change any of the underlying functionality.

- ideally we would be using something that is as close to a standard framework as possible, with tools that will allow us to upgrade the theme with little or no effort on our part (using something like [ThemeRoller](https://themeroller.jquerymobile.com) perhaps?)

issue_title: Get string from model bug?
issue_body:
I think there might be a bug in the z3 c api for extracting strings. Basically I have a string constraints corresponding to something like:

```
if (strchr(p1,'A')
      && strchr(p1,'B')
      && strchr(p2,'D')
      && strchr(p2,'E')
      && strlen(p1) == 4
      && strlen(p2)  == 4
      && strcmp(p1,p2) == 0
      )
```
where p1 and p2 are abstract buffers. And I want z3 to give me back values for p1 and p2. I'm building the query with C api and then dumping it with `Z3_solver_to_string(builder->ctx, theSolver)` to get smt2 queries: [z3.smt2.txt](https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/files/1748737/z3.smt2.txt) and with `Z3_open_log` to give you the actual C api interaction: [z3.log.txt](https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/files/1748736/z3.log.txt).

When I run the `z3 z3.smt2` (the output of `Z3_solver_to_string` + check-sat + get-value), I get:
```
sat
((AB_serial_1_version_0 "EDBA\x00")
 (AB_serial_2_version_0 "EDBA\x00"))
```
 
for the last query. Which is *good*, because both abstract buffer are some permutation of BDEA and are equal.

However when I extract the contents of the two abstract buffers in the program, with something like:
```
Z3_model_eval(builder->ctx, 
              theModel, 
              Z3_mk_const(builder->ctx,
                          Z3_mk_string_symbol(builder->ctx,
                                              "AB_serial_1_version_0"),
                          Z3_mk_string_sort(builder->ctx)),
              Z3_TRUE, 
              &out);
printf("AB_serial_1_version_0 : %s",Z3_get_string(builder->ctx,out))
```
I get
```
AB_serial_1_version_0: BEAD\x00
AB_serial_2_version_0: BAED\x00
```

Which looks suspiciously right, but the strings are *not* equal. Actually the order can change, but it always contains the right letters.

So it seems the bug is either in my use of Z3 C api or in the API itself. Does the  [z3.log.txt](https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/files/1748736/z3.log.txt)  suffice for you to reproduce this, without me finding a small C program that has this property?

issue_title: It seems that unexpected result returned in floating-point constraints
issue_body:
Hi, the conflict floating-point constraints seem to be solved as satisfiable. The constraints are as followed:
```
1) !(y>0.0)
2) fabs(x-y)<M, where M is a big floating-point number.
3) x >=0.0
4) sqrt(x-y) != 0.0
5) sqrt(x-y) !=0.0  && 2.0 * sqrt(x-y) == 0.0
```
, and  from the fifth constraint, we known the constraints are `unsat`.  But, we get the 'sat' returned, the constraints are translated as SMTLIB format as following:
```
(declare-fun symbol0 () (_ FloatingPoint 11 53))
(declare-fun symbol1 () (_ FloatingPoint 11 53))
(assert
 (not (fp.isInfinite symbol0)))
(assert
 (not (fp.isNaN symbol0)))
(assert
 (not (fp.isInfinite symbol1)))
(assert
 (not (fp.isNaN symbol1)))
(assert
 (let ((?x14 (fp.sub roundNearestTiesToEven symbol0 symbol1)))
 (let ((?x28 (fp.sqrt roundNearestTiesToEven ?x14)))
 (let (($x9 (and (distinct ?x28 ((_ to_fp 11 53) RTZ +zero)) true)))
 (and $x9 (fp.eq (fp.mul roundNearestTiesToEven ((_ to_fp 11 53) RTZ 1.0 1) ?x28) ((_ to_fp 11 53) RTZ +zero)))))))
(assert
 (let ((?x14 (fp.sub roundNearestTiesToEven symbol0 symbol1)))
 (let ((?x28 (fp.sqrt roundNearestTiesToEven ?x14)))
 (and (distinct ?x28 ((_ to_fp 11 53) RTZ +zero)) true))))
(assert
 (fp.geq symbol0 ((_ to_fp 11 53) RTZ +zero)))
(assert
 (fp.leq (fp.abs (fp.sub roundNearestTiesToEven symbol0 symbol1)) ((_ to_fp 11 53) RTZ 1.9999999999999997779553950749686919152736663818359375 1023)))
(assert
 (not (fp.gt symbol1 ((_ to_fp 11 53) RTZ +zero))))
(check-sat)
```
In the translation, we used api `distinct()` for `neq` for floating-point constraints.

issue_title: Do not segfault on unknown function declaration in Z3_get_decl_kind
issue_body:
The API function [`Z3_get_decl_kind`](https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/blob/d30ba3f1adfb84d00b5db660d30da4748a28ff6a/src/api/api_ast.cpp#L901) segfaults when a function declaration is passed that is not known to this method. This is very problematic when traversing formulas, especially because Z3 itself creates formulas that contain functions that trigger these segfaults (example in #669, but there are more than that, for example `bit2bool`).

There are also several cases where the function returns `Z3_OP_UNINTERPRETED` as a default value, where I am not sure whether this is valid.

So I would like to ask you, to please never let this method segfault, and to never let it return a wrong answer.

I suggest to either make sure that all functions that are created by Z3 are known to this method, or to return some error code, or at least to add a value like `Z3_OP_UNKNOWN` and return this as default.

Builder instead of link for read-then-write use case
issue_body:
Feature Request

Currently Brain Observatory files can be read by using a legacy type map, and written back out as 2.0 compliant files by creating a second NWBHDF5IO object.  However, the file that is generated a collection of external links, and doesn't move the data over.  Implementing a copy option would allow the use of this read/write functionality as a 1.x to 2.0 migration too.

Code snippet to start:
```
from allensdk.core.brain_observatory_cache import BrainObservatoryCache
import allensdk.brain_observatory.stimulus_info as si
import pynwb

brain_observatory_cache_manifest_file = '~/boc/manifest.json'

ophys_experiment_id = 570014520
boc = BrainObservatoryCache(manifest_file=brain_observatory_cache_manifest_file)
exp = boc.get_ophys_experiment_data(ophys_experiment_id)
legacy_map = pynwb.legacy.get_type_map()
io2 = pynwb.NWBHDF5IO(exp.nwb_file, extensions=legacy_map, mode='r')
nwb_data = io2.read()

io2 = pynwb.NWBHDF5IO('tmp.nwb', extensions=legacy_map, mode='w')
io2.write(nwb_data) 
```

issue_title: angr implementation project: Concrete Syscall Engine
issue_body:
I'm opening this issue to be able to send a link to people interested in an angr implementation project.

One of the biggest limitations of angr, when analyzing real-world software, is the environment model.
Having a simprocedure for every library function is unrealistic, so library code ends up being executed, and this library code uses system calls that angr doesn't support.
Having a simprocedure for every system call will also be pretty difficult.

One solution is some sort of layer that executes system calls concretely, on an actual kernel, and synchronizes the results into angr.
This isn't easy either, but we can discuss it in this issue.

I can see several routes that we can take:

1. A SimEngine that takes the syscall args from the SimState and them into appropriate functions of the `os` module. This is similar to what usercorn did when it was written in Python. This is the closest to the SimProcedure situation, since we'd have to implement a pass-through for each system call. However, these passthroughs would be significantly simpler than simprocedures are. IIRC, most usercorn pas
sthroughs were literally a single line of code. We might even be able to abstract _that_ away for many syscalls.
2. A SimEngine that creates a hollow process on the actual OS and uses whatever debugging interface to do system calls in it and observe the results.
3. A SimEngine that interacts with QEMU-user to monitor a stub process as it does system calls. This would be limited to QEMU-compatible
 OSes.
4. A SimEngine that spins up a QEMU-system and injects a process into it to do syscalls. This would be limited to QEMU-compatible OSes (and would probably require a disk image in the `Project`).

There are some common challenges among these:

- We'll need to concretize the arguments. That's easy to do in the base case, but we could get fancy and have the concretization attempt to produce valid inputs. I would not do this -- just wanted to mention the possibility.
- We'll need to sync the syscall results back into the state. This might be simpler for a QEMU-based option than a debugger-based option, since the former could allow us to hook appropriate events (mapped memory, etc).
- The results of a syscall might not make sense without prior syscalls (i.e., a `read` is quite different after an `open`).
- The results of a syscall will probably have side-effects on the underlying system. If your SimulationManager has two states, and one of them deletes a file, that file should still be available to the second state. The only ideas I have for addressing this at the moment is either by forking a QEMU-system process (thus keeping an associated OS state for each symbolic state) or by isolating our hollow process in a Linux namespace with filesystem layers (like docker).


Slightly related project: https://github.com/angr/angr/issues/574, though that one is a generalization of the concretization we do for unicorn, not an expansion of it into syscalls.

issue_title: RemoteProtocolError when an expecting client bails out
issue_body:
We get a request with `Expect: 100-continue`, like those that curl is known for sending:

```
import h11
conn = h11.Connection(our_role=h11.SERVER)
conn.receive_data(b'POST / HTTP/1.1\r\n'
                  b'Host: example\r\n'
                  b'Content-Length: 9001\r\n'
                  b'Expect: 100-continue\r\n'
                  b'\r\n')
assert isinstance(conn.next_event(), h11.Request)
```

But we decide we don’t want to handle it:

```
conn.send(h11.Response(status_code=403,
                       headers=[]))
conn.send(h11.EndOfMessage())
```

The only sensible thing for the client to do in this situation is to close the connection:

```
conn.receive_data(b'')
conn.next_event()
```

But that gives us:

```
h11._util.RemoteProtocolError: peer closed connection without sending complete message body (received 0 bytes, expected 9001)
```

This is not a protocol error, this is exactly what the 100-continue dance was designed for.

I’m not sure what is the right thing to do here. Maybe the current behavior is a good simplification. Or maybe h11 could accept the `ConnectionClosed` event from the client when `our_state in [h11.SEND_BODY, h11.DONE]`.

issue_title: Add head.fontRevision record to report output
Investigate possible removal of "filter" subsystem
issue_body:
In Ganga we have a system called 'filter' ([Filters.py](https://github.com/ganga-devs/ganga/blob/develop/python/Ganga/GPIDev/Base/Filters.py)) which seem to be a way of injecting code before assignments happen on schema items.

For example, setting
```python
j.postprocessors = FileChecker()
```
will have a filter (defined in IPostProcessors.py) intercede, and wrap the `FileChecker` object in a `MultiPostProcessor` so that doing
```python
type(j.postprocessors)
```
gives `Ganga.GPIDev.Base.Proxy.MultiPostProcessor`.

This is work which would make so much more sense being implemented in a standard Python `@property` 'setter' inside the `Job` class.

Apart from that, filters seem to provide the possibility of doing
```python
j.backend = 'Remote'
```
and have it look up the plugin automatically but I'm unsure that anyone uses this in reality.

In summary, I feel that the filter system is too magical and scatters the code that is doing the interception throughout the code base. We need to consider whether the feature is worth the complexity and perhaps consider it for removal in the future.

issue_title: Allow overrides for mutual authentication and hostname when using kerberos auth
issue_body:
When using Kerberos auth with a server behind a CNAME or load balancer it is necessary to set the hostname_override setting in the kerberos auth object passed to requests.  

It is also nice to be able to set the mutual authentication option to OPTIONAL as this is not always supported.

Currently there is no nice way to override these settings from the configuration file but it looks like it should be relatively easy to add in.

issue_title: Sporadic errors, only workaround is to physically reconnect camera
issue_body:
| Required Info                         |                                                                |
|---------------------------------|------------------------------------------- |
| Camera Model                       | D400 | 
| Firmware Version                   | 05.08.09.00 | 
| Operating System & Version |   Ubuntu 16 | 
| Kernel Version (Linux Only)    | 4.4.0-116-generic | 
| Platform                                 | Intel NUC |
| SDK Version                            |  2.10.0 | 


Hi,

I'm running a system with a number of RealSense cameras. We've been using the R200 and are now migrating to the R400 series.

About 50% of the time after system boot the cameras do not work.

Sometimes I get:
rs2_create_device(info_list:0x7fb4f8566930, index:0): libusb_bulk_transfer(...) returned LIBUSB_ERROR_IO Last Error: Resource temporarily unavailable

And other times there's no reply from the cameras at all and I can see in the sys log:
usb 3-4: device not accepting address 10, error -71

Only way to solve the problem is to reconnect the camera (sometimes several times). Once properly connected the camera seems to be working fine.

I'm on a Ubuntu server system that has been upgraded from the old librealsense to the new one (for the new devices). 

I've tested R415 and R435 cameras. Several of each so it's not a defect device. We're on Intel NUCs, I've tried several of those too. I'm using the original USB cables. Tried with and without USB3 hub. With or without external power to the USB3 hub. I've tried using only one camera connected, same problem.

Any idea what could be wrong? Any ideas on what I should try?

issue_title: Adjust response_handlers for Pulp3 needs.
issue_body:
As part of changes required to adjust Pulp-Smash to fit Pulp3 needs, it will be necessary to adjust the callback function, `response_handler`. These functions are used together with `api.Client` mainly.

Pulp2 and Pulp3 differs in the way the responses for certain actions are sent back. 

In Pulp3 the update of a data in a certain repository will generate the follow **202** response.

```
   {
        "_href": "http://pulp3.dev:8000/api/v3/tasks/4fc2e1fe-2564-4818-9b0d-4116a32ec2d0/",
        "task_id": "4fc2e1fe-2564-4818-9b0d-4116a32ec2d0"
    }
```

Using the` api.json_handler` as response_handler to handle this response, it will generate an error, since it is not ready to parse this pattern of response. 

Error will occur in the function [poll_spawned_tasks](https://github.com/PulpQE/pulp-smash/blob/394d3510d052660d8ed378cc919861a18e2f7400/pulp_smash/api.py#L346).

There are basically 2 choices, create other functions to handle Pulp3 response, or adapt the current ones to verify what version of Pulp is under test and perform different steps as needed.

Possibly changes to CLI Client`s `callbacks`` will be required as well, but not identified at the moment.

issue_title: Fiddle with multi-line docstrings
issue_body:
From https://github.com/PulpQE/pulp-smash/pull/20#discussion_r43385240:

> the recommended way for multiline docstring is like example below ( see https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0008/#documentation-strings for more information):
>
> ```python
> """Return a foobang
>
> Optional plotz says to frobnicate the bizbaz first.
> """
> ```
>
> In other words, the closing `"""` should stay on its own line and don't need a blank line before it.

This makes sense. Let's implement this new format throughout the application. Making this change has value because it'll make the code base more palatable to other developers who are familiar with PEP8 (and PEP257).

issue_title: Test that multiple checksums for packages in the erratum are handled correctly
issue_body:
In Pulp 2.9+ multiple checksums for packages in the erratum should be published correctly.
If the checksum of the [distributor checksum_type](http://docs.pulpproject.org/plugins/pulp_rpm/tech-reference/yum-plugins.html#yum-distributor) is available for the package in the erratum pkglist then such checksum will appear in the published updateinfo.xml, otherwise package will be listed without checksum in the updateinfo.xml.

Example 1:
- distributor checksum_type is 'sha256'
- in the erratum pkglist 'md5' and 'sha256' are available for each package

=> 'sha256' will be used in the updateinfo.xml.

Example 2:
- distributor checksum_type is 'sha1'
- in the erratum pkglist 'md5' and 'sha256' are available for each package 

=> no checksum will be published in the updateinfo.xml.

In Pulp 2.10+ a new distributor option `updateinfo_checksum_type` is introduced. If it is specified, such checksum type will be used for packages in the erratum pkglist. If it is not specified or the requested checksum type is not available for the packages in the erratum pkglist, the distributor checksum type will be used. If there is no checksum of the distributor checksum type, then no checksum will be published along with the packages in the erratum pkglist.

Example 3:
- updateinfo_checksum_type is 'md5'
- distributor checksum_type is 'sha256'
- in the erratum pkglist 'md5' and 'sha256' are available for each package

=> 'md5' will be used in the updateinfo.xml. 

Example 4:
- updateinfo_checksum_type is 'sha1'
- distributor checksum_type is 'sha256'
- in the erratum pkglist 'md5' and 'sha256' are available for each package

=> 'sha256' will be used in the updateinfo.xml. 

Example 5:
- updateinfo_checksum_type is 'sha1'
- distributor checksum_type is 'sha256'
- in the erratum pkglist 'md5' is only available for each package

=> no checksum will be published in the updateinfo.xml.

Redmine issue for handling multiple checksums: https://pulp.plan.io/issues/2020
Redmine story to support `updateinfo_checksum_type`: https://pulp.plan.io/issues/1878

issue_title: Figure out how to parallelize tests
issue_body:
Pulp Smash takes a while to execute, and it'd be nice if we could reduce the running time. One way to accomplish this is to parallelize tests. If parallel tests are desired, then two problems need to be resolved:

* Which test cases can be run in parallel?
* How does the test runner actually *run* parallel tests?

As for the first question, it's good to realize that many test cases perform global actions. For example, a test case might:

* Completely reset Pulp, including resetting services, recreating a database and removing files from the filesystem. (See `reset_pulp()`.)
* Reset individual services. (See `reset_squid()` and the `ServiceManager` class.)
* Remove all orphans.
* Munge the filesystem of the target system.
* More?

Any test case which is to be run in parallel must either be refactored to not do these things or must be marked as requiring serial execution.

As for the second case, the unittest test runner from the standard lib doesn't provide any facilities for parallelization. Maybe it can be done with unittest, but I'm unclear on how. We might need to whip up something interesting with a library like [testtools](https://testtools.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html), or we might need to pick a different test runner.

Specifically, something like this is what I'm doing:
```python
class MyObjectTypeOptions(ObjectTypeOptions):
    '''
    Options for subclasses of `MyObjectType`
    '''
    # a few class attributes initialized to `None`
    pass

class MyObjectType(ObjectType):

    @classmethod

        _meta = MyObjectTypeOptions(cls)
        # initilize attributes of the `MyObjectTypeOptions` instance
        default_resolver = default_resolver or cls._default_fields_resolver
        super().__init_subclass_with_meta__(_meta=_meta, default_resolver=default_resolver, **options)

    @classmethod
    async def _default_fields_resolver(cls, attname, default_value, root, info, **args):
        '''
        pseudo code...
        '''
        response = await my_aiohttp_client_session.get('/some/url/')
        # use the data in `response` to resolve the value for the field `attname`
        setattr(root, attname, response['somekey'])
        return response['somekey']
```
Essentially, I'm resolving field values from the response of some REST API, using aiohttp's async client utilities. But then I get this error:
```python

issue_title: Add option for configuring number of proxies for end user IP calculation
issue_body:
# HTTP header injection problem in current Axes implementation

At the moment we implement a naive default of using the first IP address in `X-Forwarded-For` address list for calculating the end user IP address. In fact, this should depend on the number of proxies between the server and the client. For example, if we are running behind a nginx proxy or an [AWS Elastic Load Balancer](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/elasticloadbalancing/latest/classic/x-forwarded-headers.html), the proxy *appends* the client address to the *end* of the list of given `X-Forwarded-For` headers. This leads to the current version of Axes having a [XFF spoofing vulnerability](https://www.whitehatsec.com/blog/top-3-proxy-issues-that-no-one-ever-told-you/).

We should provide a configurable value for setting the number of proxies that would default to `0` proxies if not set; [this would be compatible with the current behaviour](https://github.com/jazzband/django-axes/blob/master/axes/decorators.py#L65).

For example, let's say the proxy and client supply the following headers:

### Case 1:  a trusted proxy appends client IP with no preset when we have one (1) known proxy in the chain

    X-Forwarded-For: ip-appended-by-trusted-proxy

### Case 2: two (2) known proxies inserting the client IP into the chain

    X-Forwarded-For: original-client-ip-by, ip-appended-by-trusted-proxy

### Case 3: client injects (spoofs) a malicious X-Forwarded-For header into the request when we have one (1) trusted proxy

    X-Forwarded-For: 127.0.0.1, ip-appended-by-trusted-proxy

### Case 4: client injects (spoofs) a malicious X-Forwarded-For header into the request when we have two (2) trusted proxies

    X-Forwarded-For: 127.0.0.1, original-client-ip-by, ip-appended-by-trusted-proxy

### Discussion

So, we have a bunch of different scenarios and we can have any other header configured as the client IP denominator, such as `X-Real-IP` by nginx of the default `X-Forwarded-For` which is often used with Apache, nginx, ELB, and the like.

If we could configure the IPs we could implement the following and count the IP addresses from the end of the list:

```python
BEHIND_REVERSE_PROXY = getattr(settings, 'AXES_BEHIND_REVERSE_PROXY', False)
REVERSE_PROXY_HEADER = getattr(settings, 'AXES_REVERSE_PROXY_HEADER', 'X-Forwarded-For')
NUM_PROXIES = getattr(settings, 'AXES_NUM_PROXIES', 0)
header = request.META[REVERSE_PROXY_HEADER]

def get_ip(
        request, 
        num_proxies=NUM_PROXIES, 
        header_name=REVERSE_PROXY_HEADER,
        behind_reverse=BEHIND_REVERSE_PROXY
):
    """Calculate client IP from given request with configured headers and number of proxies
    
    For example, the following could be used 
    with X-Forwarded-For configured to '127.0.0.1, 10.10.10.10'

        num_proxies=1, 
        header_name='X-Forwarded-For'
    """
        
        ip == [addr.strip() for addr in request.META[header_name].split(',')][-num_proxies]
            == [addr.strip() for addr in request.META['X-Forwarded-For'].split(',')][-1]
            == [addr.strip() for addr in '127.0.0.1', '10.10.10.10'].split(',')][-1]
            == ['127.0.0.1', '10.10.10.10'][-1]
            == '10.10.10.10'

    if not  behind_reverse:
        ip = request.META['REMOTE_ADDR']
    else:
        header = request.META[header_name]
        addresses = [addr.strip() for addr in header.split(',')]
        ip = addresses[-num_proxies]

    return ip
```

So, we could then use the more secure version to calculate the end user IPs:

```python
# Case 1
>>> get_ip('ip-appended-by-trusted-proxy', num_proxies=1)
'ip-appended-by-trusted-proxy'

# Case 2
>>> get_ip('original-client-ip-by, ip-appended-by-trusted-proxy', num_proxies=2)
'original-client-ip-by'

# Case 3
>>> get_ip('127.0.0.1, ip-appended-by-trusted-proxy', num_proxies=1)
'ip-appended-by-trusted-proxy'

# Case 4
>>> get_ip('127.0.0.1, original-client-ip-by, ip-appended-by-trusted-proxy', num_proxies=2)
```
This would still work with e.g. `X-Real-IP` header like `X-Real-IP: client-ip-by-nginx` and with settings set to something `AXES_NUM_PROXIES = 0` OR `AXES_NUM_PROXIES = 1` and `AXES_REVERSE_PROXY_HEADER = 'X-Real-IP'`, so I think all existing functionality would work correctly with the setup. Also, fetching the `-0` IP from the IP address list is equivalent to fetching `0`, so all existing apps would be unaffected unless configured with new `AXES_NUM_PROXIES` settings.

The functionality is implemented in configurable fashion in [Django REST Framework](http://www.django-rest-framework.org/api-guide/throttling/#how-clients-are-identified) and other software such as Werkzeug, and [Django middleware exists with XFF correction](https://github.com/codetry/xff).

issue_title: Add method remove_if to builder with signature build -> bool
issue_body:
The README shows the following example for filtering builds:
```python
    filtered_builds = []
    for settings, options, env_vars, build_requires, reference in builder.items:
        if settings["compiler.version"] != "4.6" and settings["build_type"] != "Debug":
             filtered_builds.append([settings, options, env_vars, build_requires])
    builder.builds = filtered_builds
```

IMO, for short ones, it would be more readable to be able to write something like the following: 

```python
builder.remove_if(lambda build: build.setting["compiler.version"] != "4.6")
```

For slightly longer ones, would like to write something like:
```python
def is_gcc_46_debug(build):
    return build.setting["compiler.version"] != "4.6"` and build.settings["build_type"] != "Debug"

`builder.remove_if(lambda build: is_gcc_46_debug(build))`
```

I think it's not too hard to implement.  I don't know if we can even do `build.setting` or `build["setting"]`, nor do i know anything about named builds.  I was going to try to implement myself quickly until I saw that, so it will take some extra time to see how that works.  Please just let me know if there are any significant barriers to this one.

issue_title: Agregar opción para mover `theme` a `keyword` en `push_dataset_to_ckan`
issue_body:
**Contexto**

Los temas específicos (`theme`) son temas que tienen sentido dentro del contexto y dominio temático bajo el cual cada nodo original opera. Sirven para clasificar a sus datasets según categorías temáticas específicas a la problemática que aborda ese nodo, pero que tienen una granularidad demasiado alta para la generalidad de los datos de toda la Administración Pública.

El `theme` de un dataset en su nodo original, es una extensión del `keyword` de ese dataset en el nodo integrador (son etiquetas).

**Implementar**

`pydatajson` tiene que hacer un _downgrade_ de los `theme` originales hacia el rol de etiquetas (`keyword`) en el nodo integrador.

Agregar a la función `push_dataset_to_ckan` un argumento opcional `theme_to_keyword` con default `True`.

Este flag mueve (los _elimina_ de un lado y los escribe en otro) todos los elementos originales del array `theme` en la metadata original de un dataset y los agrega al `keyword` del dataset que va a ser empujado a un CKAN.

**Notas**

Esto es una operación de transformación de los metadatos originales cuyo sentido es preservar el uso exclusivo de temas transversales (evitando el uso de temas específicos) de un dataset en un contexto de portal diferente (con un público y alcance temático transversal, mientras que el nodo original tiene un público y alcance temático específico).

**Entregable**

La nueva opción en `push_dataset_to_ckan`, junto con sus tests asociados  y las modificaciones correspondientes a la documentación en los docs de RTD.

issue_title: Simplify generated API method implementations
issue_body:
Instead of creating an api_call every time there is a call, create an api_callable object for each API method in the constructor, and pass the request and options to that api_callable. For example, existing implementation of publisher_api.create_topic:

```
        req = pubsub_pb2.Topic(name=name)
        settings = self._defaults['create_topic'].merge(options)
        api_call = api_callable.create_api_call(self.stub.CreateTopic,
                                                settings=settings)
        return api_call(req, metadata=self._headers)
```

Would change to:

```
        req = pubsub_pb2.Topic(name=name)
        return self._create_topic_callable(req, options)
```

New initialization code needs to be added to the constructor:

```
        self._create_topic_callable = api_callable.create(
            self.stub.CreateTopic,
            settings=defaults['create_topic'])
```

As for headers, they could be plumbed through `defaults` instead of passed individually to each api_callable. 

As for page streaming, the api_callable would need to dynamically determine whether page streaming would be on or off based on the options.

issue_title: Visdom & Pillow-simd
issue_body:
I am using pillow-simd, which is a drop-in replacement for pillow that offers a speedup for certain operations by using SIMD operations of your CPU.  
However, when installing visdom, it installs pillow, which overwrites my pillow-simd install.

They solved this in pytorch/vision, by checking if pillow-simd is installed on the system in __setup.py__.
```python
from pkg_resources import get_distribution, DistributionNotFound

def get_dist(pkgname):
    try:
        return get_distribution(pkgname)
    except DistributionNotFound:
        return None

requirements = [ ... ]
pillow_ver = ' >= 4.1.1'
pillow_req = 'pillow-simd' if get_dist('pillow-simd') is not None else 'pillow'
requirements.append(pillow_req + pillow_ver)
```

I think the only drawback of using this, is if you provide wheels, as it would then be unable to determine whether you use pillow-simd or not.  
I guess this is no concern here right?

issue_title: Docs reorganization
issue_body:
I've been walking through the documentation and had a few notes.  I'd be happy to make these changes but wanted to check in before I submitted anything.  

The separation between single machine and distributed learning seems odd to me.  Many of the topics listed in single machine (grid search, pipelining, possibly even incremental learning) are still relevant when on a cluster.  

I might instead flatten the TOC to just remove the single-machine/distributed distinction, and give all of the subsections their own home.  This might flatten the TOC to something like the following:

1.  Pipelines
2.  Hyper-parameter search
3.  Incremental learning
4.  Generalized Linear Models
5.  Joblib
6.  XGBoost
7.  Clustering
8.  Examples
9.  API

I've also found it pleasant recently to start sections with the API relevant for that section.  [Futures docs](http://dask.pydata.org/en/latest/futures.html) for an example.  This gives a quick list at the beginning of each section on the API relevant for that section.  Those functions still link to the main API doc page.

Any thoughts or objections to this reorganization @TomAugspurger ?

Some extra TODOs now that we're using numba

- [ ] Update setup.cfg to build universal wheels
- [ ] Update dependencies on conda-forge recipe

issue_title: better docs for `--expand` flag
issue_body:
I just had to get a new certificate.  i had `a, b, c, d` and needed to add `e, f`.

I knew I needed a new certificate, but I could not figure out how to cancel or keep the first certificate from rewing. 

I could not figure out how to `add` names to a certificate.

the docstring on `letsencrypt-auto` was not very helpful.  the faqs weren't either.

I eventually found a recommendation to use `--expand` on a forum post.  it was exactly what was needed.

I don't think the command needs to change, but the docs and faq should make this a lot more apparent.  I was very close to doing a revoke+create

issue_title: Should the client revert temporary and in progress changes during the next run?
issue_body: If the Certbot exits with backups that weren't finalized in a checkpoint, the client will attempt to restore these backups on the next run. Is this the correct behavior? While largely mitigated by #814, it can theoretically cause issues like the one described in #940 and #1456.

issue_title: Error message for empty domain name is incorrect
issue_labels: [{'id': 276239890, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/certbot/certbot/labels/easy-fix', 'name': 'easy-fix', 'color': '207de5', 'default': False}, {'id': 272789317, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/certbot/certbot/labels/error%20handling', 'name': 'error handling', 'color': 'eb6420', 'default': False}, {'id': 149596829, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/certbot/certbot/labels/Good%20Volunteer%20Task', 'name': 'Good Volunteer Task', 'color': 'fef2c0', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
In a recent commit that [Fix FQDN checks](https://github.com/certbot/certbot/commit/6156b452bcf7c924f12c334e7094ff5a9049ee37),
 the error message fails to address the case where domain name is an empty string. See below.

`--domain` is a CSV string, but the 2nd DNS name is parsed to be an empty string, which caused 
and error message `Requested domain  is not a FQDN because label  is too long.`. 

It would have been more logically to say `Requested domain "" is not a FQDN because label  is either empty or too long.`

The check it failed is here: https://github.com/certbot/certbot/blob/6156b452bcf7c924f12c334e7094ff5a9049ee37/certbot/util.py#L432

``` bash
$ docker run --rm -it --entrypoint bash quay.io/letsencrypt/letsencrypt:latest
root@4b80271cdd6c:/opt/certbot# certbot certonly \
>     --webroot \
>     --non-interactive \
>     --agree-tos \
>     --email test@test.com \
>     --webroot-path /var/lib/letsencrypt \
>     --domain www.example.com,,test1.example.com \
>     --no-verify-ssl \
>     --debug \
>    --test-cert
Requested domain  is not a FQDN because label  is too long.
```

issue_title: Kwikapi source code
NFC data transfer too slow using nfcpy
issue_body:
Hi guys,
I have written a python application, based on nfcpy, that transfers a binary file onto a type 2 tag.
So far everything worked fine besides the transfer speed. On windows the transfer rate is around 200Bytes/second and on Linux slightly better, 280Bytes/second whereas I would expect a closer values to the theoretical value of 800Bytes/second.
I have stripped completely my python script and my Tag code, so basically I can't blame any of the two applications. 
When debugging I do see that the messages take quite a while to write, around 0.8 seconds per record, which is quite a lot. My records have 150 bytes.
So at this point I am inclined to blame the USB driver or the nfcpy which are the only black boxes in this setup.
I have used two different card readers, the Sony S380 and the ACR122, the results on the Sony are slightly better, maybe because it uses a newer version of the PN5XX (PN533) and the ACR122 uses the PN532.
Regarding the drivers I have been using the native drivers, which don't work, and the lisbusb v1.2.6.0, libusbkcv3.0.7.0 which give the above results.
On Linux, the performance increases a bit, to around 250bytes/second, still far from the intended speed.
