      File "<string>", line 1, in <module>
      File "tmp/pip-build-pp4a_V/lz4/setup.py", line 22, in <module>
        liblz4_found = pkgconfig.installed('liblz4', LZ4_REQUIRED_VERSION)
      File "tmp/python-lz4/venv/lib/python2.7/site-packages/pkgconfig/pkgconfig.py", line 155, in installed
        raise ValueError(msg)
    ValueError: >= 1.7.5 is not a correct version specifier

```


## Possible Solution

Improve error handling around `pkgconfig.installed()` call in `setup.py`.

```diff
diff --git a/setup.py b/setup.py
index 73a8e2a..2cd413a 100644
--- a/setup.py
+++ b/setup.py
@@ -21,8 +21,9 @@ else:
     try:
         liblz4_found = pkgconfig.installed('liblz4', LZ4_REQUIRED_VERSION)
         py3c_found = pkgconfig.installed('py3c', PY3C_REQUIRED_VERSION)
-    except EnvironmentError:
+    except Exception:
         # Windows, no pkg-config present
+        # or pkgconfig call failed
         pass
```

Maybe both `liblz4_found` and `py3c_found` should be set to `False` too.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. `pip install pkgconfig=1.3.1`
2. `<pkg-manager> install liblz4-dev`
3. edit `liblz4.pc` file, replace version with `Version:  "1.5.r128"`
4. attempt to `pip install lz4==2.0.0`

## Context 

Before ~ 2016 `liblz4` used slightly unconventional versioning schema `...->125->126->127` see
https://github.com/lz4/lz4/blob/r128/lib/liblz4.pc.in

The library was repackaged internally using https://github.com/lz4/lz4/blob/r128/cmake_unofficial/CMakeLists.txt that constructs versions in the format of `X.Y.r###`, e.g. `1.5.r128` in the `liblz4.pc` file:

```
Name: lz4
Description: fast lossless compression algorithm library
URL: http://code.google.com/p/lz4/
Version:  "1.5.r128"
Libs: -L${libdir} -llz4
Cflags: -I${includedir}
```

Python `pkgconfig` module `installed()` [call](https://github.com/matze/pkgconfig/blob/v1.3.1/pkgconfig/pkgconfig.py#L127) fails with `invalid literal for int() with base 10: 'r128'`, because of the assumption it makes about the version format [here](https://github.com/matze/pkgconfig/blob/v1.3.1/pkgconfig/pkgconfig.py#L42)

issue_title: Implement a padding helper for hashlock secret encoding
issue_body: One of the problems addressed in #391 was that the secret in the test [(`'secret'`) didn't match the (32 byte) padded secret in the smart contract](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/pull/391/files#diff-9161c3328a41e5b779ebb4d7966b8abcR53). This lead to a `throw` which naturally is hard to debug. To avoid similar problems in the future, we should introduce a helper function `utils.create_hashlock(secret)` that applies the specified padding. Then we should rigorously remove all usage of plain `sha3(secret)` in tests (and elsewhere).

issue_title: Cross asset transfer support
issue_body:
-  User Alice wants to transfer N of asset X to user Charlie for at most M of asset Y
-   Alice and Charlie both only have an account for on of the assets

Solution New MediatedAXTransfer message which additionally specifies: target_asset, target_amount. Every hop checks the channel graph if there is path with a node which has accounts for both assets. A node with both assets would accept the source_asset and forward the transfer as the target_asset, if it agrees with the source_amount, target_amount ratio. As with the MediatedTransfer, we'll have optimistic routing i.e. as there is no global view on the transfer capacity (deposit/balance) and the exchange rate of the nodes. If a node can not successfully transfer, it reports this back to the calling node which tries the next best path.

issue_title: reuse results from external tasks
issue_body: Right now sos takes existing results if a `.res` file exists. However, it is possible that output files have been removed so it seems necessary to check if it the output files (at least their signatures) exist before ignoring a step.

issue_title: automatic variable sharing with random jump.
issue_body:
Currently automatic variable sharing is implemented by following the kernel jump. However, there are cases where kernel jump are random which makes automatic variable sharing difficult. For example, 

If there are existing cells with type python and sos,

```
cell python
a= 1
```

when we jump to the following cell, sos does not know that we are jumping out of python so `a` is not updated...

```
cell sos
a
```

issue_title: stdout and stderr of master task
issue_body:
Right now each task writes to its own `.out` and `.err` file. This works ok for single tasks but can be troublesome for master tasks that execute multiple tasks in parallel.

Currently each subtask will write to their own `.out` and `.err` files so the master `.out` and `.err` files will be empty, and no error will be reported if one or more subtasks fail. Allowing all subtasks to write to the master `.out` and `.err` files is also problematic because of lock/race condition etc.

I think a proper solution would be collecting all `.out` and `.err` files after subtasks have been completed.

issue_title: test run mode
issue_body:
We have two options for debugging remote tasks:

1. dryrun mode, which executes all tasks in dryrun mode, which print scripts without executing it. 
2. We have run mode with `-J1` which submit only 1 task to the server. However, after the first task fails, it will continue to submit the rest of the tasks to the server.

I am wondering if we should introduce a `test` or `test-first` mode in which only one task is executed,

1. the rest will not be submitted if the first one fails
2. the rest will all be submitted (not -J1) if the first one succeed

The problem is there is no easy way to define "succeed" because a task can fail at different stages. For example, a task can fail at as early as task creation stage, or as late as running for a few minutes....

issue_title: Settable parameters as context managers
issue_body:
It might be useful for parameters which can be set to act as context managers, such that user code can be guaranteed that a parameter is returned to its original value after a block of code. For instance:

```python
>>> v = dac.voltage()
>>> with dac.voltage(-1):
...     # Do stuff with the DAC output set to -1 V.
...
>>> assert abs(dac.voltage() - v) <= tolerance
```

issue_title: Creating 9 million static pages - how to?
issue_body: I am trying to find a solution to a problem and wondered if frozen-flask could be that solution. My company has been asked to create a lot of pages for a travel site, nearly 9 million (!) that are the same template but contain different data taken from an API call. We'd ideally like to serve them statically because of the expensive API call and to reduce server load. Now to query the API to fetch the data to generate the template takes in order of 0.1 of a second on average so it could take 10 days or something to create all the pages! So my question is: is Frozen Flask only designed to create the static pages in "a oner" or can it be configured somehow to chew through the page creation in a batch as a job or can it go live like a regular Flask app and when a page is requested a static version is created and the next time that url is queried the static version is served?

issue_title: ray.wait should not consume current worker
issue_body:
The following never finishes:

```python
import ray
import time

ray.init(num_cpus=1)

@ray.remote
def sleep(x):
    time.sleep(x)

@ray.remote
def delegate(x):
    ray.wait([sleep.remote(x)])

ray.get(delegate.remote(30))
```

The issue is demonstrated because there is only one CPU available, so the `sleep` task cannot run because there is no available worker. Surprisingly, the following does work:

```python
@ray.remote
def delegate(x):
    ray.get(sleep.remote(x))
```

It seems that inside `ray.get` Ray is smart enough to release the current worker to the `sleep` task, but for `ray.wait` it is not. I would ask that the same should happen for `ray.wait`. It is clear that the task is not doing anything (just waiting) so it should not be consuming a resource/worker.

Tested with Ray version 0.5.0.

issue_title: Consider replacing ray.get_gpu_ids() with a more general ray.get_env().
issue_body: Right now, we expose the IDs of the GPUs that a task may use via `ray.get_gpu_ids()`. However, we may want to replace that with a more general `ray.get_env()` which returns a dictionary containing the key `"GPU_IDS"`. issue_title: Starting processes by hand.
issue_body:
To debug crashes, it is often useful to start each of the processes (the plasma store, the plasma manager, the local scheduler, the global scheduler, redis, etc) by hand. This way, some of the processes can be started in gdb, and logging messages from the different processes are easier to read.

Currently, this can be done as follows (run all of these commands in separate terminal windows).

1. Start Redis
```
rm dump.rdb; ./src/common/thirdparty/redis/src/redis-server
```
2. Start the global scheduler (and pass in the Redis address)
```
./src/global_scheduler/build/global_scheduler -r 127.0.0.1:6379
```
3. Start the plasma store
```
src/plasma/build/plasma_store -s /tmp/s1 -m 1000000000
```
4. Start the plasma manager
```
src/plasma/build/plasma_manager -s /tmp/s1 -m /tmp/m1 -r 127.0.0.1:6379 -h 127.0.0.1 -p 23894
```
5. Start the local scheduler
```
src/photon/build/photon_scheduler -s /tmp/sched1 -p /tmp/s1 -m /tmp/m1 -r 127.0.0.1:6379
```
6. Modify `start_ray_local` in `lib/python/ray/services.py` to be something like this.
```python
def start_ray_local(node_ip_address="127.0.0.1", num_workers=0, num_local_schedulers=1, worker_path=None):
  if worker_path is None:
    worker_path = os.path.join(os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__)), "workers/default_worker.py")
  redis_port = 6379
  redis_address = address(node_ip_address, redis_port)
  object_store_names = ["/tmp/s1"]
  object_store_manager_names = ["/tmp/m1"]
  local_scheduler_names = ["/tmp/sched1"]
  address_info = {"node_ip_address": node_ip_address,
                  "redis_port": redis_port,
                  "object_store_names": object_store_names,
                  "object_store_manager_names": object_store_manager_names,
                  "local_scheduler_names": local_scheduler_names}
  # Start the workers.
  for i in range(num_workers):
    start_worker(address_info["node_ip_address"],
                 address_info["object_store_names"][i % num_local_schedulers],
                 address_info["object_store_manager_names"][i % num_local_schedulers],
                 address_info["local_scheduler_names"][i % num_local_schedulers],
                 redis_port,
                 worker_path,
                 cleanup=True)
  return address_info
```
7. Then start Python, and run
```python
import ray
ray.init(start_ray_local=True, num_workers=2)
```
Then try running some commands and see which processes crash! Processes can be started in gdb as well (or lldb on Mac).

issue_title: Refactor required in query logic?
issue_body:
In staff.py, ticket_list (line 742) the logic says;
`queue, id = query.split('-')`

however in public.py, line 81 the logic is
`parts = ticket_req.split('-')`
`queue = '-'.join(parts[0:-1])`
`ticket_id = parts[-1]`

The latter accounts for queue names with a dash in them. The former does not.

I haven't tried it yet, but i would infer from this that looking up a ticket in a queue with a dash in the queue's slug would not be possible if you were logged in (and therefore exercising the code in staff.py)

Is it worth refactoring this logic into the ticket object, something like 
`@class method`
`def queue_and_id_from_query(query):`
`parts = query.split('-')`
`queue = '-'.join(parts[0:-1])`
`return queue, parts[-1]`

@register
class StageEndpoint(Endpoint):

filtersets.py:
```
class MiddlewareSpecFilter(FilterSet):
    """ this is the base filter
        based on the intermediate table middlewarespec
        to filter specific attributes that are related to middlewarespecification """
    middleware = filters.CharFilter(label='Middleware',
            name='middlewarespecification__middleware__name',

    stage = filters.CharFilter(label='Stage',

class StageFilter(MiddlewareSpecFilter):

        fields = ('middleware',

issue_title: Suggestion: M3U support.
issue_body:
I recently downloaded and set up your bot for local media playback for audio files but Red doesn't currently have the flexibility i'm looking for, so for now, i'm just using Virtual Audio Cables. 

Problem: When configuring Red's media directory, I noticed that it could only play media from one sub-folder down. So my first line of thought was.. 'hmm maybe i can keep my music organized where it was, and just create a singular directory full of M3U files for Red to read from that way it can play media from everywhere on my Music HDD regardless of what directory the music was actually located in.' <-- this train of thought just didn't work.

Potential Solutions:

1.) Implement M3U extensions so I can create a folder for playlist files without having to copy all of my music into one folder, which leads to me running out of HDD space or me completely messing up the order my albums are arranged in.

2.) Completely redo the code and have it to where it can search your media via reading ID3 tags of any mp3 files located in ALL sub-directories of the media folder that you set up when you set Red up.

I think the first solution would work the best, short term. Long term goal would be #2 as it would probably require a lot of work. Can you guys implement M3U support?

issue_title: Ingesting with executor multiproc seems to just idle around
issue_body:
### Expected behaviour
The ingester should ingest data when it is run with the option `--executor multiproc 2`.

### Actual behaviour
The ingester does not ingest data and is idling (i.e. no noticeable CPU load). The status message does not indicate any progress, it always says "completed 0, failed 0".

When the first 3 tasks are submitted, the following error is shown:
```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/home/user/anaconda3/envs/cubeenv/lib/python3.6/multiprocessing/queues.py", line 234, in _feed
    obj = _ForkingPickler.dumps(obj)
  File "/home/user/anaconda3/envs/cubeenv/lib/python3.6/multiprocessing/reduction.py", line 51, in dumps
    cls(buf, protocol).dump(obj)
BufferError: could not get scalar buffer information
```
For all the other tasks, that are submitted, the error is not shown.

### Steps to reproduce the behaviour

```
$ datacube -v ingest -c "ingestion_config" --executor multiproc 2
```

The ingestion configuration I am using is derived from this [Landsat 8 example](https://github.com/ceos-seo/agdc-v2/blob/master/ingest/ingestion_configs/landsat_collection/ls8_collections_sr_lake_chad_example.yaml).

### Environment information

* Which ``datacube --version`` are you using?
```shell
$ datacube --version
Open Data Cube core, version 1.5.5
```
* What datacube deployment/environment are you running against?
Debian 9.5 with Anaconda3 5.2.0, conda 4.5.9

### Additional Notes
When leaving out the executor argument, ingesting data works, however, it is slow.

Just a thought: Might it be possible, that some component is waiting here for some tasks to finish but these tasks will never finish because of an error?

issue_title: Mapping from `Dataset` to `Storage Unit` is poorly defined when using netcdf storage
issue_body:
When using netcdf files for storage we often "stack" year worth of observations into one netcdf file. As a result multiple `dc.Dataset`s point to the same file on disk. Since `dc.Dataset` represents a single point in time only one slice along the time dimension "belongs" to a given dataset. Which slice that is, is poorly defined.

We do not store time slice index anywhere in the `dc.Dataset` metadata document. Instead `center_time` property is used to "locate" the index that "matches best". 

The "matches best" logic is fairly involved, see:

https://github.com/opendatacube/datacube-core/blob/5f7211d2eb26ac10d6dfb754418d4bd3079cdf7a/datacube/storage/storage.py#L473-L501

It can not use exact equality since `center_time` is a `datetime.datetime`, which can contain sub-second component, while what's stored on disk is `float64` containing seconds since 1970, and is always rounded to seconds (I only observed it being rounded down, but who knows, it happens inside netcdf library). By the way this process is repeated for every band being loaded, even though they all share the same time slice index.

It means that file needs to be opened before one can figure out what part of the file is to be read. This is a problem for optimized read scheduling.

issue_title: Default `requests` timeouts are too short and can't be configured easily
issue_body:
The default is 10s for `read` and 60s for `connect`, neither of which makes much sense considering that the server is going to be an AWS API Gateway which has a timeout of 30s max. We should default to 40s for read (so the server has a chance to send the 504 back in case it times out) for read and 10s for connect. I don't see why a longer connect timeout is necessary.

This should also be easier to configure. I would think via environment variables so it can be changed at runtime and hanging the default timeout off the client instance rather than `_ClientMethodFactor`.

issue_title: MPIExecutor fabric should report heartbeats
issue_body:
There's no mechanism in the MPIExecutor to detect failure of individual instance of the mpi fabric. The fabric could die due to walltime expiration or any MPI failures and the executor should be able to detect such failures so that tasks can be re-launched by the DFK.

For this to work we would need:
1. Periodic heartbeats from the fabric:rank0 to the executor
2. Heartbeats to include task_ids currently being processed
3. Executor to monitor heartbeats to identify status.

issue_title: Operator version updates are not necessary for BC-compatible changes
issue_body:
It was recently pointed out to me that what I have been telling people about operator versioning, and what Versioning.md says, do not line up.

From the spec:

> Once an operator is published, all implementations of that operator's domain, op_type, op_version MUST adhere to the signature and semantics of the operator at the time of publication. Any change of semantics implies a new operator, which MAY share a domain and op_type with another operator. This includes adding new behavior triggered only by previously unspecified inputs or attributes. These changes in semantics also MUST have a distinct operator id.

From what I have been saying:

> I'm not convinced that BC version bump here was necessary. We're simply adding a new attribute, and its default value (0) matches the old behavior, right? So this is a backwards-compatible change and no version update is necessary. (Of course, it is also semantically valid to do a version update; it just means that you're forcing producers to rev their targeted operator version to take advantage of the new version. Best not to make their life hard.)

I was under the impression we had agreed that BC-compatible changes wouldn't require revving the version (which is a somewhat disruptive thing to do, since no one gets the new behavior until they bump the operator spec version they're targeting.) I'm going to submit a PR to fix the wording here.

CC @donbox

issue_title: Missing Relu in DenseNet
issue_body: In the official DenseNet implementations ([caffe](https://github.com/liuzhuang13/DenseNetCaffe/blob/master/make_densenet.py#L57), [torch](https://github.com/liuzhuang13/DenseNet/blob/master/models/densenet.lua#L43), [pytorch](https://github.com/gpleiss/efficient_densenet_pytorch/blob/master/models/densenet.py#L153)) and others ([mxnet](https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/blob/master/python/mxnet/gluon/model_zoo/vision/densenet.py#L105), [torchvision](https://github.com/pytorch/vision/blob/master/torchvision/models/densenet.py#L222)) a Relu activation is applied after the [last batch norm](https://github.com/keras-team/keras-applications/blob/master/keras_applications/densenet.py#L221) and before the global pooling, however this activation does not exist in this keras implementation.

issue_body: Currently Simpson.h has a NR-based routine for integration by Simpson's rule, which Gary modified so it's written as a template with the integrand as the template argument.  We should replace this with some alternative, such as Mike's integration package.
